item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
I want to like this more, but unless it’s in the Broken Masquerade canon or in another where the Foundation has public facing policies, I just can’t see them boarding up a whole city and no one from the government or citizens trying to break in

Furthermore, the article doesn’t really explain what happened to the people who lived in the city

It’s a fun gag, but I don’t know why you invest so much in describing the otherworldly delivery service just to bail on it for the joke referencing Calamity Jane and Bill Hickock

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: The ghosting pun, which is really the only thing distinguishing the second half of this from any other one-sided romance, is practically forced into the reader's face with zero subtlety, and is remarkably unfunny.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
There are a number of problems with this:

  • The description has a number of frustrating breaks from even the concept's internal logic:
    • The "humanoid entities" are described to be in "good physical condition" even though they're not human or any other recognizable species. So what does "good physical condition" even mean? Why even bring this up?
    • The first collapsible says that the items recovered from the first package that you describe are a salt shaker and rum bottle. Then you have a note, but there's no context for the note- was it also found in the package or something? You never say so.
  • And then you appear to abandon the concept of creepy-nonhuman-package-delivery-system altogether in favor of an epistolary presentation of a one-sided romance between Calamity Jane and Wild Bill Hickok. Not that there's anything wrong with the romance being a concept to write about, but it's a very sudden and jarring change of direction that doesn't flow from or follow from what came before it.
  • There's also a very jarring history disconnect, which interferes with suspension of disbelief. Calamity Jane grew up in Virginia City _Montana_, not Nevada (and, as far as I know, neither Jane nor Wild Bill ever visited Nevada). And yet you go all-in on making this about Jane and Wild Bill having a connection to Virginia City, Nevada. Anyone who has any familiarity with their story will find the centrality of Virginia City, Nevada in this piece to be jarring. Imagine if someone were trying to write a Stephen King homage about kids facing a supernatural horror in Portland, only to find that the story is set in Portland, Oregon rather than Maine.

-1

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I like ghosts and I like puns — this has both. +1.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I think you've done a great job here. Not only have you created interesting creatures, but you've also built an interesting story around them, telling us just enough to keep is hooked but not so much as to ruin the mystery. It has a very interesting tone as well. Well done!
